heroHeadline: NATO ALLIES MULL SKIPPING 2028 SUMMIT TO AVOID TRUMP; SIPRI: EUROPE MILITARY SPENDING SURGES 14% TO $864B — FASTEST GROWTH SINCE COLD WAR; MERZ: IRAN 'HUMILIATING' TRUMP
heroSubtitle: On April 28, Reuters reported that NATO allies — citing six sources — are in active discussions about abandoning the annual summit format specifically to avoid confrontational encounters with Trump, with the 2028 summit (Trump's final full calendar year) the most likely to be skipped. The discussion, in which Secretary General Rutte holds final authority, reflects the depth of the institutional crisis: NATO's own member states are recalibrating their meeting schedule around their most powerful member's unpredictability rather than their collective security priorities. Simultaneously, SIPRI's 2026 global military spending report confirmed European military expenditure rose 14% in 2025 to $864 billion — the largest single-year European military spending increase since the Cold War — with SIPRI analysts explicitly attributing part of the surge to allies' diminished confidence in automatic US Article 5 commitment. All 32 NATO members now meet the 2% GDP target for the second consecutive year. Germany's 2026 defense budget hit a record €108.2 billion under Chancellor Merz, who also issued the sharpest German rebuke of Washington's strategy since the rift began, declaring that Iran is 'humiliating' the Trump administration. These developments follow the most compressed escalation week in NATO's 77-year history: Trump's 'The Atlantic Alliance has failed us' declaration (Apr 26), Macron's Athens statement that EU Article 42.7 is 'stronger than Article 5' (Apr 26), and NATO's three sequential legal clarifications that no suspension mechanism exists in its founding treaty (Apr 25–27). The Helsingborg Foreign Ministers' meeting (May 21–22) and Ankara Summit (July 7–8) remain the next institutional anchors.
footerNote: Data sourced from NATO HQ, White House, US Department of Defense, Reuters, Bloomberg, Wall Street Journal, SIPRI, Newsweek, Modern Diplomacy, The Economist, The Telegraph, TIME, Politico, German Federal Foreign Office, Axios, NPR, Washington Post, Al Jazeera, EU News, Swedish MFA, France 24, Euronews, CNN, PBS NewsHour, Gulf News, US Senate Foreign Relations Committee, GB News, Welt am Sonntag, Turkish Minute, CFR, Stars and Stripes, Defense News, Xinhua, Foreign Policy, Eurasian Times, The Defense Post, AP, CBS News, Europa Press, Atlantic Council, Middle East Monitor
